channel.title: Privacy on Level Up Your Code | Web & App Development Tutorials
channel.description: Recent content in Privacy on Level Up Your Code | Web & App Development Tutorials

item.title: Best VPN for Privacy in 2026: Honest Review (No Sponsored BS)
item.description:
Here’s something that bugs me: most VPN reviews are basically paid ads. The VPN industry throws a ton of money at affiliate marketing, and that’s why every “best VPN” article ranks whoever pays the highest commission at #1.

I’m not doing that here. This review is based on actual privacy features, how transparent the company is, and whether they’ve been independently audited — not how much they pay per referral.

item.title: Best Password Managers in 2026 Compared: Which One Should You Actually Use?
item.description:
Still using the same password on multiple sites? Or maybe saving everything in Chrome and hoping for the best? Yeah, that’s a problem waiting to happen.

A password manager is probably the single best thing you can do for your online security. But picking one can be confusing — there are a lot of options, and they all claim to be the best.

I’ve used most of the popular password managers over the past few years. Here’s what I actually think about each one.

item.title: Best Self-Hosted Apps to Replace SaaS You're Paying For (with Docker Compose)
item.description:
Every month I used to look at my credit card statement and see a long list of SaaS subscriptions. $10 here for monitoring, $15 there for analytics, $5 for a password manager, another $12 for file storage. None of them were expensive on their own, but together they added up fast.

At some point I started wondering: could I just run this stuff myself?

Turns out, yes. And it’s way easier than it used to be. Docker Compose made self-hosting dead simple — what used to take hours of manual configuration now takes a single YAML file and one command.

item.title: Stop Reusing Passwords A Practical Guide to Password Managers
item.description:
If you reuse passwords, the internet is quietly stacking odds against you. One small site gets breached, your email and password leak, and attackers try the same combo on your email, banking, cloud storage–everywhere. That “I’ll remember it” system works right up until it doesn’t. The fix isn’t superhuman memory; it’s outsourcing the problem to a tool designed for it: a password manager.

What a password manager actually does

  • Generates strong, unique passwords for every account
  • Stores them encrypted, synced across your devices
  • Auto‑fills only on the correct websites/apps
  • Audits your vault for weak/reused/compromised passwords
  • Holds secure notes, TOTP codes (in some apps), and sometimes passkeys

The goal is simple: every account gets its own high‑entropy secret, and you never type or remember it again.
